Study Finds Moderate Exercise Linked to Improved Sleep Quality

A new study published in the Journal of Sleep Research found that adults who engaged in 30 minutes of moderate exercise three to five times per week reported improved sleep quality compared to sedentary individuals.

The study, conducted by researchers at the University of Queensland, followed 1,200 participants over 12 months. Participants were divided into three groups: a control group with no exercise intervention, a moderate exercise group (brisk walking, cycling), and a high-intensity exercise group.

Results showed that the moderate exercise group reported a 23% improvement in sleep quality scores on the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, while the high-intensity group showed a 19% improvement. The control group showed no significant change.

Dr. Sarah Chen, lead author of the study, noted several limitations. "Our sample was predominantly urban, educated adults aged 25-55. We cannot generalize these findings to all populations," she said. "Additionally, sleep quality was self-reported, which introduces potential bias."

The researchers recommended that future studies incorporate objective sleep measurements such as polysomnography and expand the demographic range of participants.

The study was funded by the Australian Research Council and had no industry conflicts of interest.
